hero_summary: Ajam is one of the core maqamat in modern Arabic practice. Although it may appear close to the Western major mode, Arabic treatment of it remains maqamic rather than purely Western-harmonic.
short_identity: A clear and ascending maqam whose phrases rely on brightness and structural clarity, but which should not be reduced to a Western major scale.

quick_facts.karar: Depends on the chosen transposition; in common modern practice it may be Rast (C)
quick_facts.ghammaz: The fifth as a strong middle point of repose
quick_facts.upper_jins: Usually extends into the upper Ajam region, with possible colorings depending on context and repertoire
quick_facts.character: Bright, clear, direct in structure, and strong in ascent
quick_facts.common_start: It usually begins by establishing the Ajam jins clearly before any additional coloring

sayr.summary: In Arabic music, Ajam is not treated as merely a Western major scale, but as a maqam with its own way of functioning and moving. It usually begins by establishing the Ajam jins clearly, then ascends gradually toward the upper region while confirming the fifth as a strong point of repose, reaches the jawab with clarity, and then returns downward either to the tonic or to the main resting degree used in the performer’s chosen formulation.
resting_tones: The tonic according to the transposition being used; if transposed on Rast (C), then Rast (C) is the tonic
The fifth: a strong central point of repose and expansion in ascent
The upper tonic: a natural point of arrival when the ascending phrase completes itself
The Ajam tone itself may serve as a final center of repose in certain older formulations
motion_notes: Ajam depends on clarity of structure more than on ornamental ambiguity
Its ascent is usually clear, bright, and gradual
There is a major difference between dry scalar ascent and maqamic ascent with stabilization, emphasis, and melodic breathing
The return in Ajam should be clear and well-shaped so that the maqam’s identity remains stable
common_path: Ascend gradually and clearly without rushing into extra coloring
Rest on the fifth as a main point of expansion
Return decisively through a middle resting point to the tonic

common_modulations: Expansion into the upper Ajam as the maqam’s natural continuation
Possible passage to neighboring ajnas depending on school and repertoire
Ajam is not understood from two fixed ajnas alone, but from the routes between ajnas and the points of emphasis and return
One must be careful not to reduce it to a straight major scale with no distinct maqamic logic

examples.title: Al-hubb sultanuh qasi
examples.note: One of the early full-length treatments in Maqam Ajam, showing the maqam’s Arabic identity

examples.title: Qum wa nadim
examples.note: An example cited by al-Khula‘i in his description of Maqam Ajam

examples.title: Shanbar
examples.note: Another example cited by al-Khula‘i in Maqam Ajam

reference_recordings.title: Al-hubb sultanuh qasi
reference_recordings.note: An important reference for hearing Ajam in the Arabic sense rather than as a purely Western major

reference_recordings.title: Qum wa nadim
reference_recordings.note: A traditional reference cited by al-Khula‘i in connection with Ajam

reference_recordings.title: Shanbar
reference_recordings.note: A traditional reference among the examples of Maqam Ajam

reference_recordings.title: Man li-sabbin fi al-hawa
reference_recordings.note: An example connected with Ajam Ushayran, useful for distinguishing it from Ajam itself
